one_line_takeaway: A 16-factor structured system/user-prompt framework for local LLM chain-of-thought reasoning yields only modest DDoS-detection accuracy gains (~1-5%) but substantially larger, human-rated gains in reasoning transparency and evidence grounding (up to ~40% in small models, 8-12% in large models), suggesting structured prompting mainly buys auditability rather than raw classification performance.
key_finding: Across all 12 models and both prompt-authoring conditions (Manual and ChatGPT-generated), the structured framework (FW) improved every metric relative to NoFW, but the size of the gain differed sharply by metric type. Classification accuracy gains were modest and shrank with model size: e.g. gemma-2B accuracy rose 69.8% to 72.6% (Manual, +4.0%) / 71.6% to 75.0% (ChatGPT, +4.8%), while ChatGPT-5.1-scale accuracy rose only 91.0% to 92.0% (Manual, +1.1%) / 92.4% to 93.3% (ChatGPT, +1.0%). By contrast, human-rated reasoning-quality scores showed much larger relative gains, especially for small models: gemma-2B Evidence score rose from 0.72 to 1.05 (Manual, +45.8%) and Structure from 0.76 to 1.12 (+47.4%), whereas Llama-70B and ChatGPT showed smaller but still consistent gains (roughly 5-9%). The paper summarizes this as reasoning-quality improvements of 'up to 40%' in smaller models and 8-12% even in larger models, versus accuracy gains of only ~1-5% overall. Ablation showed removing evidence-grounding factors caused the largest drops in Faithfulness/Evidence scores, and removing scope/dataset-grounding constraints increased unsupported assumptions during interpretation of numerical traffic features. Inter-rater reliability was strong: Cohen's kappa = 0.87 (Evidence), 0.84 (Faithfulness), 0.89 (Structure), 0.82 (Taxonomy), all above 0.80.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a 16-factor structured prompt engineering framework (F1-F16, grouped into four dimensions: Context and Scope Control, Evidence Grounding and Traceability, Reasoning Structure and Cognitive Control, and Security-Specific Analytical Constraints) that splits control between a system prompt (global constraints, output schema, calibration) and a user prompt (feature grounding, taxonomy alignment, temporal reasoning). The framework is evaluated empirically as a case study on DDoS attack detection in SDN traffic, comparing 'no framework' (NoFW) vs 'with framework' (FW) prompting across 12 local/cloud LLMs, using both manually authored and ChatGPT-generated prompt variants. The authors also introduce a human-centric reasoning-quality evaluation methodology (Evidence Grounding, Faithfulness, Structure Compliance, Taxonomy Alignment, Confidence) scored by two independent raters with Cohen's kappa reliability checks.
threat_model: This is not an adversarial/attacker-vs-defender security paper; there is no adversary model, attack surface, or defense against a malicious actor. The 'security' framing is a deployment-reliability concern: LLMs performing DDoS traffic classification/analysis for SOC-style workflows may hallucinate, drift beyond the provided evidence, or produce reasoning that cannot be audited by human analysts. The paper's concern is trustworthiness/auditability of AI-assisted security analysis (false positives/negatives, ungrounded attribution claims, unverifiable reasoning chains), not resistance to prompt injection, jailbreaks, or adversarial network traffic crafted to evade detection.
limitations: The paper explicitly states these are its own limitations/future-work items, not scoped as such in a dedicated limitations section but stated in the discussion/conclusion: (1) the case study covers only one task (DDoS detection in SDN traffic) and one dataset, with generalization to other cybersecurity tasks (intrusion detection, malware analysis) left to future work; (2) the 400-row sample size, while described as 'balanced and manageable,' is small relative to the original Kaggle dataset and was chosen for practicality of manual reasoning review rather than statistical power; (3) reasoning-quality scoring, despite dual-rater averaging and kappa reporting, is inherently subjective/ordinal rather than an automated or reference-based metric; (4) the comparison conflates two independent variables (structured-vs-unstructured framework AND manual-vs-ChatGPT-generated prompt authorship) without a full factorial ablation isolating each dimension beyond the factor-removal ablation described narratively; (5) no comparison to non-LLM baselines (e.g., traditional IDS/ML classifiers) is reported, so the absolute competitiveness of LLM-based detection versus conventional DDoS detectors is not established, only the relative FW-vs-NoFW improvement within LLMs.

datasets_used: Kaggle DDoS SDN dataset (Kazin, 2021) - 400 sampled rows, 23 columns (3 categorical, 20 numerical features), binary label (0=normal traffic, 1=DDoS attack traffic)
quantitative_results: Reasoning-quality gains reported as 'up to 40%' in smaller (2B-4B) models vs accuracy gains below 5%; 8-12% reasoning gains vs ~1-2% accuracy gains in larger (20B-70B) models
baselines_compared: NoFW (unstructured/no-framework prompting) vs FW (structured 16-factor framework prompting), for both Manual-authored and ChatGPT-generated prompt variants
12 LLM configurations compared against each other: Gemma-2B/12B/27B, Llama-3.2-3B/3.1-8B/3.3-70B, Qwen3-4B/8B/32B, GPT-OSS-20B, ChatGPT (and ChatGPT-5.1 mentioned in abstract)
No non-LLM (traditional IDS/ML classifier) baseline is reported
